metrics.narrative: Cindy Simons named real, specific pain (the quality team is 'very overwhelmed with all the data' and there is no system to process incoming supplier quality documents) but no numbers were ever attached to it. Zycus quoted its own generic stat on the call (RFP creation cut from ~40 days to 5-6 days) but this was never validated or adopted by the buyer as her business case. No cost of the current manual process, no ROI target, and no budget figure exist on record.

sources: Salesforce contact roles (single contact, no advocacy evidence)
champion.narrative: Cindy Simons is an evaluator (Manager, Procurement), not a demonstrated champion. She showed genuine interest during the call but has not advocated internally in any confirmed way since; the CFO conversation she said she'd initiate was never confirmed to have happened, and the deal went silent immediately after. No one is currently selling Zycus internally at Vvf LLC.

competition.narrative: SAP is the operative competitive threat, but as an incumbent/do-nothing alternative rather than a competing bid: Cindy asked directly, 'Why would I want to use your system versus using SAP to create and process POs?' and separately raised the possibility of building out SAP further instead of buying a new tool. Vvf LLC completed a SAP migration in March 2025, which raises the bar for justifying another new system so soon after.

identify_pain.narrative: Cindy clearly articulated two distinct pains: (1) her quality team is overwhelmed managing incoming supplier quality documents manually inside SAP with no proper system ('I don't know what quality is doing with it right now... they're very overwhelmed with all the data'), and (2) there is no supplier performance/scorecard system today ('Do we have performance management, and we don't right now'). Both are buyer-voiced, not inferred.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting mechanics have been discussed at all. The deal is far too early (single demo, Qualified stage) for any paper process to exist.

economic_buyer.narrative: Cindy named the CFO as the actual approval authority ('I think I want to take this to our CFO... give me a high level summary of what you shared with me') but the CFO has never been named, contacted, or engaged directly by the relevant stakeholder is no evidence of any CFO touchpoint in the 113 days since the last logged activity. The economic buyer is identified only by role, not by relationship.

decision_process.narrative: A rough sequence was voiced by Cindy: she reviews the materials, loops in the CFO and corporate IT, and 'our corporate would want to look at it' before any investment across the company's three plants (one US, two India) is approved. No dates, no formal steps, and no confirmation this process has actually started since Zycus never confirmed sending the promised materials.

decision_criteria.narrative: No formal evaluation criteria have been established. Cindy indicated interest in specific modules (eSourcing, contract management, supplier information management, plus supplier risk/performance for her quality team) but this is product-fit color, not agreed criteria, weighting, or a documented requirement set.

flags: Multi-thread to power: This deal is dangerously single-threaded on Cindy Simons (Manager, Procurement, Evaluator), who has no budget authority and went silent after the demo. Cindy explicitly named the CFO and corporate IT as the decision authorities on 28 January 2026. The next move is to secure a joint discovery call with Cindy, the CFO, and corporate IT (by mid-July 2026) to map the buying committee, validate the quality document pain and cost justification, assess SAP integration complexity, and identify the Economic Buyer. Until power is engaged, this deal cannot advance.

Land the SAP wedge with ROI: SAP is the entrenched incumbent (migrated March 2025), and Cindy questioned the value of Zycus over SAP on the 28 January call. The wedge is SAP's high expansion cost and lack of purpose-built supplier quality document management (the pain Cindy owns). However, the wedge was not quantified or landed. The next move is to run a lightweight ROI workshop with Cindy and the CFO (by late July 2026) that quantifies the time and cost pain of manual quality document tracking (15+ docs per vendor, overwhelmed team, email/Excel chaos) versus the cost of SAP expansion, and presents a scoped pilot (quality document management module only, 30-60 day proof-of-value) to demonstrate fast ROI and reduce change-management risk. This positions Zycus as a low-risk, high-value complement to SAP, not a rip-and-replace.

No pricing or budget validation: Cindy stated 'I don't even know what your cost is' on 28 Jan 2026. No pricing discussion, no budget range validated, no ROI quantified. Cost justification versus SAP (incumbent) required before CFO proposal.
